text: The surgeon places an indocyanine green (ICG) tattoo at the site of a small lesion to enhance its visibility during the robotic ultra-low anterior resection for low rectal cancer.
The surgeon focuses on the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) and performs a high ligation, isolating and securing the vessel.
The surgeon creates a window in the tissue and uses a vessel sealer to transect the exposed structures.
The surgeon continues lateral dissection, separating tissue layers along the lateral aspect of the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies the left-sided plane to gain a comprehensive view around the mesorectum, facilitating the dissection process.
The surgeon proceeds with dissection down the upper part of the left side of the total mesorectal excision (TME), focusing on this area as it appears accessible.
The surgeon performs posterior dissection, focusing on the tissue planes behind the mesorectum.
The surgeon maintains dissection within the precise anatomical plane known as the 'holy plane,' ensuring clear separation of tissue layers during the procedure.
The surgeon follows along the right lateral aspect of the mesorectum, continuing the dissection in the specified plane.
The surgeon identifies the ICG tattoo marking on the tissue, which is visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon performs the total mesorectal excision (TME) dissection using a monopolar cautery device, focusing on precise tissue separation.
In the lateral location, the surgeon encounters crossing middle hemorrhoidal vessels, which require the use of bipolar cautery or a vessel sealer for precise management.
The surgeon uses monopolar scissors to perform the dissection, effectively manipulating tissue without the need for additional instruments.
The surgeon attempts to connect the right lateral dissection to the anterior dissection, and similarly connects the left lateral dissection, using monopolar cautery.
The surgeon defines the dissection plane and releases the mesorectum, providing proper traction for ongoing circumferential dissection towards the levator ani muscle floor.
The uterus is suspended to the abdominal wall using a Keith needle, enhancing exposure for deeper dissection during the colectomy procedure.
The surgeon enters the rectovaginal septum, carefully dissecting and removing the mesorectum while preserving the integrity of the vagina.
The surgeon aims to dissect two to three centimeters beneath the visible ICG tattoos on the tissue surface.
The surgeon continues circumferential dissection around the rectum, connecting the right and left lateral dissections to the anterior aspect, while maintaining traction on the mesorectum.
The surgeon carefully dissects around the mesorectum, maintaining an even circumferential approach to ensure uniform separation of the tissue.
The surgeon navigates below the tattoo markings placed at the level of the tumor, indicating the area for precise dissection.
The surgeon lifts the mesorectum away from the underlying levator muscles, revealing the anatomical separation between these structures.
The surgeon operates with four laparoscopic ports visible in the abdominal wall, performing the procedure without an assistant.
The surgeon switches the laparoscopic camera to arm number two, adjusting the view within the surgical field.
The camera provides a straight-on view into the deep pelvis, revealing anatomical structures relevant to the colectomy procedure.
The surgeon prepares to use a 12mm trocar in arm number three for stapling, considering the position of the anastomosis for potential diversion.
The surgeon extracts the specimen through the ileostomy site, minimizing the risk of hernia by limiting the incision to this single location.
The surgeon focuses on detaching the levator muscles from their attachments to the mesorectum, using precise dissection techniques.
The camera is positioned in arm number two, providing a view of the surgical field.
The surgeon ensures the dissection is positioned as low as desired below the tumor in the deep pelvis, using visual cues to confirm the location.
The surgeon transitions from using their finger to inserting a scope into the surgical field.
The surgeon carefully dissects and mobilizes the entire mesorectum, preparing to staple flush with the levator muscles.
The surgeon introduces a 45mm stapler into the surgical field, positioning it to staple from anterior to posterior orientation.
The surgeon checks the surgical field to ensure that the vagina has not been inadvertently grasped during the procedure.
The surgeon uses a surgical instrument to extend across the targeted area, completing the transection.

The surgeon demonstrates the dissection of the proximal mesentery, approaching the cephalad aspect of the inferior mesenteric artery, with the chosen proximal margin located in the proximal sigmoid colon. The redundant sigmoid and descending colon are visible, ensuring adequate reach.

The surgeon uses cautery to mark the planned margin on the colon, preparing to exteriorize the segment and place an anvil for anastomosis due to the low position preventing suturing from the nominal side.
The surgeon inspects the dissected plane, observing the separation of tissue layers achieved during the procedure.
The transected rectum is visible within the levator crease, following the stapling process that was not captured on camera.
